coredata.dc:title: Effect of macropores in titanium dioxide layer on the enhancement of photovoltaic conversion efficiency of long-persistence-phosphor enhanced dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Shi Qiang
Chen, En Zi
Cheng, Jun Di
Gu, Xiu Yun
Wei, Kun
Sun, Geng Zhi
Gao, Xiu Ping
Pan, Xiao Jun
Zhou, Jin Yuan
Xie, Er Qing
coredata.dc:description: Mesoporous TiO2 (m-TiO2) have been often employed to improve the photovoltaic conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). While when incorporated with a long-persistence-phosphor (LPP) layer, only a limited PCE enhancement was obtained for the resultant DSSCs. This is mainly due to that little incident sunlight can transport through the solid m-TiO2 photoanode films and then enter and excite the LPP layer. Here in this work, tunable amount of macropores were introduced into hierarchically porous TiO2 (h-TiO2) using polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as pore-forming agent. As a result, the optimized DSSCs with h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes show the highest PCE of 8.05%, which is improved by 29.00% and 14.51%, compared to those with m-TiO2 and m-TiO2/LPP ones, respectively. Series of analysis indicates that the macropores benefit the transmission of incident sunlight through the h-TiO2 layer and reduce the light reflection at the interface of h-TiO2/LPP. Furthermore, the carbon-doping in TiO2 caused by PVP shifts up the Fermi level of TiO2 and the interface of h-TiO2/LPP enhances the separation of photo-generated carriers, both of which increase the open circuit voltage of the DSSCs. In addition, the DSSCs with h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes can well work in dark with a real PCE of 57.37%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-sensitized solar cells
Titanium dioxide
Macropore
Mesopore
Long persistence phosphor
Photovoltaic conversion efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology optimization and characterization of h-TiO2
Crystalline structures and chemical bonds of h-TiO2 NPs
N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and UV–vis spectra of h-TiO2 NPs
Optimization on PVP addition for h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes
Performances of h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes in dark

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-e) SEM images of h-TiO2-n NP films (n = 0, 5, 8, 10 and 15), and (f) EDS spectrum of h-TiO2-8 NPs.
(a,c) TEM images of h-TiO2-0 and h-TiO2-8 NPs, and (b,d) their corresponding HR-TEM images.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) XPS full spectra and (d) C 1 s XPS spectra of h-TiO2 with different PVP additions.
(a) N2-adsorption isotherms, (b) pore size distributions, (c) macro/meso pores’ relative volumes, and (d) UV–vis spectra of h-TiO2-n NPs, respectively.

(a) J-V curves, (b) Nyquist plots, (c) Bode plots, and (d) τ n-V OC curves of the DSSCs based on h-TiO2-0, h-TiO2-8 and h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes under simulated sunlight illumination (AM 1.5, 100 mW cm−2). The inset in (a) is the cross-sectional SEM image of h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes, the inset in (b) represents an equivalent circuit for the DSSCs.

(a) J–V curves for the DSSCs based on h-TiO2-n/LPP photoanodes under AM 1.5 simulated sunlight illumination, (b) J–V curves for the DSSCs based on h-TiO2-n/LPP and h-TiO2 photoanodes in dark. The inset in (a) shows its partially enlarged details.

Schematic illustration of (a) fabrication procedure of DSSCs based on h-TiO2/LPP/N719 composite photoanodes, and (b-d) structure of h-TiO2@N719, h-TiO2@N719/LPP, and P25@N719/LPP photoanodes, and transport path of photons and electrons in them.
Photovoltaic parameters of the DSSC with h-TiO2-0, h-TiO2-8, h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes.
Photovoltaic parameters of the DSSC with h-TiO2-n/LPP photoanodes under AM 1.5 simulated sunlight illumination.
Photovoltaic parameters of the DSSC with h-TiO2-n/LPP photoanodes in dark.

head.ce:title: Effect of macropores in titanium dioxide layer on the enhancement of photovoltaic conversion efficiency of long-persistence-phosphor enhanced dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shi Qiang Kang and En Zi Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mesoporous TiO2 (m-TiO2) have been often employed to improve the photovoltaic conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). While when incorporated with a long-persistence-phosphor (LPP) layer, only a limited PCE enhancement was obtained for the resultant DSSCs. This is mainly due to that little incident sunlight can transport through the solid m-TiO2 photoanode films and then enter and excite the LPP layer. Here in this work, tunable amount of macropores were introduced into hierarchically porous TiO2 (h-TiO2) using polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as pore-forming agent. As a result, the optimized DSSCs with h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes show the highest PCE of 8.05%, which is improved by 29.00% and 14.51%, compared to those with m-TiO2 and m-TiO2/LPP ones, respectively. Series of analysis indicates that the macropores benefit the transmission of incident sunlight through the h-TiO2 layer and reduce the light reflection at the interface of h-TiO2/LPP. Furthermore, the carbon-doping in TiO2 caused by PVP shifts up the Fermi level of TiO2 and the interface of h-TiO2/LPP enhances the separation of photo-generated carriers, both of which increase the open circuit voltage of the DSSCs. In addition, the DSSCs with h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes can well work in dark with a real PCE of 57.37%.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have attracted much attention in the past two decades due to their abundant raw materials, low cost, and simple processes [1–6]. However up till now, the DSSCs’ photovoltaic conversion efficiencies (PCEs) are still facing serious challenges before their real applications [7,8]. Those limited PCEs are often due to the bad light harvesting of solar light that is mainly associated to the mismatch between the photons’ energy and the band gap of photoanode materials or dye molecules [9]. As far as the traditional TiO2/N719-dye photoanodes are concerned, the N719 dye molecules contribute to most of the light harvesting in visible light region (especially the light near 520 nm), and the TiO2 components only contribute a little to the light harvesting in ultraviolet (UV) one [10–13].

Thus, to broaden the range of spectral absorption, one strategy was proposed to down convert the high-energy photons into the low-energy ones that are suitable for the absorption of N719 dye molecules via efficient inorganic phosphors.[14–16]. And one of the most common and effective routines has been demonstrated to construct a back scattering phosphor layer between the photoanode and the electrolyte. Kim et al. [17] have incorporated an up-converting β-NaYF4:Yb3+,Er3+ phosphor microcrystal layer to convert infrared light to visible ones, and obtained an improvement of 19.29% in PCE. And Tang et al. [18] have constructed a down-converting long persistence phosphor (LPP) back scattering layer into TiO2-based photoanodes, and found that the green LPP (SrAl2O4:Eu2+) shows the highest improvement of 24.75% in PCE. While, He et al. have obtained an overall improvement of only 13% in PCE for another green LPP materials (SrAl2O4:Eu2+,Dy3+) [19]. More recently, the authors found that the thickness of LPP layers shows a great influence on the transport of electrolyte ions and photo-generated electrons towards TiO2/N719 layers [20]. And similarly, the properties of the TiO2/N719 layers should also show a great influence on the function utilization of LPP layers. Often, the size of the used TiO2 nanostructures is of the scale of less than 100 nm, while the size of the commercial LPP particles is of several micrometers. Then, a large difference exists between the sizes of the TiO2 and LPP layers, causing a strong light reflection at their interface [21–25]. As a result, few amount of light can enter the LPP layers, leading to a low utilization of down-converting fluorescence effect. Therefore, it is still instructive to explore the effect of TiO2 layer on the LPP’s function utilization for further improvement in PCE of DSSCs.

In this work, a type of hierarchically porous TiO2 nanoparticle h-TiO2 NP) films with different pore size distributions was designed to investigate the effect of pore sizes on the utilization efficiency of LPP and the resultant improvements in PCE of DCCSs. Results showed that the introduced macropores in the TiO2 layer will not only increase the transport of electrolyte ions through the photoanodes, but also increase the function utilization of LPP materials. The DSSCs based on h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes show an overall PCE of 8.05%, which is further improved by 14.5% compared to that based on meso-porous TiO2 (m-TiO2)/LPP ones. Furthermore, the h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes also show the highest PCE of 57.37% in dark.

Tetrabutyl titanate, acetic acid and nitric acid were bought from Rionlon, Tianjin, China Corp., China; polyethylene glycol (PEG, M w ≈ 20,000), octoxinol (OP-10), from Aladdin Reagent Inc., China; polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, M w ≈ 1300,000) from Merck Inc., Germany; P25-type TiO2 powder from Degussa GmBH, Germany; LPP (SrAl2O4:Eu2+) powder from Shenzhen HuiDuoSheng Luminous Materials Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals are of analytical grade, and were used as received without further purification.

The h-TiO2 NPs were prepared via a sol-gel method combining with a hydrothermal process, as illuminated in Scheme 1. First, 10 ml of tetrabutyl titanate was added drop by drop into an un-sealed glass bottle containing 100 ml of deionized water under magnetic stirring at room temperature (RT). After 30 min, a type of white powder was obtained via a successive dewatering and filtration process on the prepared suspended solution. Then, a type of mixture solution containing 10 ml of acetic acid and 1.0 ml of nitric acid was slowly added into the obtained white powder drop by drop in oil bath (80 ℃) under stirring. After 15 min, another 50 ml of deionized water was added into the mixture under stirring. And then, the glass bottle was sealed and placed under continuous stirring for 1 h, and a type of transparent TiO2 colloid was obtained.

Subsequently, the obtained TiO2 colloid was poured into a 50-ml stainless steel hydrothermal reaction kettle with Teflon inner sleeve, and heated at 200 °C for 12 h in a drying oven. When naturally cooled to RT, the treated TiO2 colloid was taken out and poured into a beaker, with addition of 1.1 g of commercial P25 TiO2 powder. After ultrasonication for 30 min, the mixture colloid was sent back to the sealed hydrothermal reaction kettle, and further heat at 200 °C for another 12 h. When naturally cooled down to RT, the TiO2 mixture colloid was again taken out and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. And the collected white TiO2 colloids were further mixed with 2.1 g of polyethylene glycol (M w ≈ 20,000), 2.6 ml of octoxinol (OP-10) emulsifier and different-content PVP (0, 5, 8, 10 and 15 wt%), and dried at 80 °C for 2 h in air. Finally, a series of white powder was obtained, respectively labelled as h-TiO2-0 (m-TiO2), h-TiO2-5, h-TiO2-8, h-TiO2-10, and h-TiO2-15.

Before the assembly, a layer of h-TiO2 NPs and a layer of LPP particles were successively coated on the clean fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) slides using the doctor blade method, following by annealing at 500 °C for 1 h to remove the PVP component. And then, the TiO2/LPP composites were loaded with the traditional N719 dyes, whose details can be reviewed from our previous work [20]. In this work, the thicknesses of TiO2 and LPP (SrAl2O4:Eu2+,Dy3+) layers were controlled to be ca. 12 and 25 µm, respectively; and the effective areas of the photoanodes are 0.24 cm2 (0.3 cm × 0.8 cm). The DSSCs were assembled with an as-fabricated TiO2/LPP/N719 photoanode, a Pt counter electrode, and the traditional I-/I3 - redox electrolyte (0.1 M lithium iodide (LiI), 0.6 M imidazolium iodide (1,2-dimethy l-3-propylimidazorium iodide), 0.05 M iodine (I2), and 0.5 M 4-tert-butylpyridine in acetonitrile) with a sandwich configuration. All reagents for the preparation of Pt counter electrodes and electrolyte were purchased from Yingkou OPV Tech New Energy Co., Ltd, China, and used as received.

The photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) and open-circuit voltage-decay (OCVD) curves of the DSSCs were measured on a solar simulator system (Zolix SS150, Beijing, China, AM 1.5 G simulated sunlight, 100 mW cm−2), and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra were obtained on an electrochemical workstation (RST5200, Shiruisi, Zhengzhou, China) from 0.01 Hz and 100 kHz at open-circuit voltage with a potential pulse of 10 mV. As for the tests of the J-V curves in dark, the DSSCs were lighted by the AM 1.5 G simulated solar light for 1 min before tests. And the used light intensities of the excited LPP layers were obtained using a standard silicon solar cell before the assembly.

The morphologies and microstructures of h-TiO2 NPs were characterized by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Apreo S, FEI Company, USA, equipped with energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS)) and a high-revolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA). The crystalline structures and chemical compositions were characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Philips, Pert Pro, Cu Kα, 0.154056 nm), a micro-Raman spectroscope (Raman, Jobin-Yvon, HR800, 532 nm), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD, UK). And the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were tested to study the specific surface area via Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis and the pore volume and pore size distribution via the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analysis (Micromeritics, ASAP2020).

As is reported, the introduce pores (especially mesopores) can well improve the PCE of TiO2-based DSSCs, because they can offer a large surface area for the adsorption of more dye molecules, and a large number of interconnected facilitated electron transport channels within the m-TiO2 films [26–28]. And suitable size of the mesopores can benefit the redox ionic pairs’ shuttle and percolation, and depress the photoelectron’s recombination at the interface of TiO2@N719/electrolyte [29]. On the other hand, the excessively increased size of pores will often lead to a decreased light absorption for the photoanode films [30,31]. What’s more, the macropores at the TiO2/LPP interface will also reduce the light reflection, due to their smaller difference in size [21]. This is to say that the introduced macropores will benefit incident sunlight (especially the UV portion) entering the LPP layer for down-conversion phosphor effect in our case, as illuminated in Scheme 1(b)−(d). Accordingly, the utilization of down-converting phosphor from LPP layers will be enhanced, improving the PCEs of the DSSCs. Besides, the macropores can also benefit the diffusion and efficient transport of electrolyte [25], which will reduce the blocking effect from the additional LPP layer in DSSCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology optimization and characterization of h-TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of h-TiO2 NPs with different PVP addition contents (i.e., h-TiO2-n, n = 0, 5, 8, 10, 15). All h-TiO2 NPs show a uniform distribution in particle size. Without PVP addition (Fig. 1(a)), the h-TiO2-0 (i.e., m-TiO2) NPs are closely connected with each other, and many compact big NP clusters were formed with a size of several hundred nanometers. Once the PVP was added (Fig. 1(b)−(e)), the h-TiO2 particle clusters’ size greatly decreases to several ten nanometers, and a large number of pores are formed in the NP films, which is similar to the published work [30]. Moreover, with the increase of PVP addition contents, the h-TiO2 NP films become looser and looser with a fluffy morphology, indicating more and more pores form within them. This porous structure will benefit the absorption of dye molecules and the transport of electrolyte ions, thereby enhance the photovoltaic performances of DSSCs. As for the formation mechanism of the pores, it could be explained by the removal and/or decomposition of PVP molecules within the samples. And the formation of mesopores could be mainly due to the removal of the PVP components coated on the P25 NPs, the macro-pores be mainly due to the removal of the excessive PVP clusters filling between the P25 NPs. Besides, as shown in Fig. 1(f), the EDX spectrum shows that the h-TiO2-8 NPs are mainly comprised of Ti, O and C. The introduced 1.85 at% C elements could be due to the decomposition of PVP compositions [32], which would increase the conductivity of the h-TiO2 NP films.

Further investigations on the microstructures of h-TiO2 NPs were conducted via HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 2(a) and (c), the h-TiO2-0 (i.e., m-TiO2) NPs show a much closer packed structure than the h-TiO2-8 NPs. As a result, the mean particle size of h-TiO2-0 NPs was estimated to be 22 nm, while that of h-TiO2-8 NPs to be 15 nm. These comparisons indicate that the addition of PVP could benefit the dispersion of nanoparticles, which further lead to the formation of pores between the NPs. Moreover, from the HR-TEM images (Fig. 2(b) and (d)), the interplanar spacing of 0.35 and 0.32 nm respectively correspond to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 and the (110) plane of rutile TiO2 [33–36]. These results indicate that the h-TiO2-0 NPs possess a mixture crystalline phase of anatase and rutile, but the h-TiO2-8 NPs mainly possess the anatase one, which could be due to the crystalline growth induction effect from PVP components [37]. Thus, both the decreased particle size and the increased anatase phase in h-TiO2-8 NPs could benefit the dye adsorption in DSSCs [10,38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystalline structures and chemical bonds of h-TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3(a) compares the XRD patterns of h-TiO2-n (n = 0, 5, 8, 10, 15) NPs. All annealed h-TiO2 NPs are a mixture of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS NO: 21–1272) and rutile one (JCPDS NO: 21–1276) [39,40]. Once the PVP was added, the signal intensity from rutile TiO2 decreases greatly. And at 8 wt% of PVP addition, the h-TiO2 NPs possess the minimum intensity of rutile TiO2, which is consistent with the above TEM observation. Moreover, via Debye-Scherrer formula, the grain sizes of h-TiO2-n NPs are estimated to be 20.1 nm (0 wt%), 18.3 nm (5 wt%), 15.3 nm (8 wt%), 14.1 nm (10 wt%) and 12.7 nm (15 wt%), implying that the PVP can hamper the growth of h-TiO2 NPs. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3(b), all four Raman peaks located at 145, 398, 518, and 640 cm−1 could be well assigned to the Eg(1), B1g(1), A1g + B1g(2), and Eg(2) modes of anatase TiO2, respectively [41–43]. And few signals from the rutile TiO2 were detected in h-TiO2 NPs.

The surface chemical bonding states of h-TiO2 NPs were studied by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the full XPS spectra indicate that all h-TiO2 are comprised of Ti, O and C. And the C signals come from the residual carbon formed during the decomposition of the PVP addition [44]. Moreover, the fine XPS spectra of C 1 s core level show three characteristic peaks centered at 282.5, 284.2, and 286.5 eV, which could be assigned to the TiC, CC bond, and CO bonds [45]. And the intensity of TiC and CC bonds gradually increases with the increase of PVP addition. Moreover, this appearance of Ti-C and CC bonds indicates that the h-TiO2 NPs might be well connected via the incorporated carbon atoms, and form a chemical bonding between them. Thus, these chemical bonds would further improve the separation and transport of photo-generated electrons in h-TiO2 layer [32,46]. Besides, combing with the analysis on the XPS spectra of O 1 s core level (Fig. S1), the chemical polarity of TiC, CO, and TiO bonds can synergistically enhance the adsorption of N719 dye molecules on the h-TiO2 NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and UV–vis spectra of h-TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: As stated above, the addition of PVP will change the porous property of TiO2 NPs. The surface porous properties of TiO2 NPs were determined using N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms. Fig. 4(a) compares N2-adsorption isotherms of h-TiO2-n NPs. And their specific surface area (SAA) was estimated via the BET analysis. As listed in Table S1, the SSA of h-TiO2 NPs first increases with increasing the PVP addition contents, and reaches its maximum value of 83.79 m2 g−1 at PVP content of 10 wt%. And then the SSA reversely decreases with further increasing the PVP addition. Fig. 4(b) presents the pore volume and pore size distribution of h-TiO2-n NPs. All samples possess two type of pores, one is the mesopores with size of 20 nm, and the other is the macro pores with size of 100 ~ 500 nm. As shown in Fig. 4(c) and listed in Table S1, with increasing the PVP content from 0 to 10 wt%, the pore volume of mesopores almost shows no change, while that of macropores increases from 0.130 to 0.235 cm3 g−1, improved by 81%. And further increase PVP content to 15 wt%, the pore volume of macropores quickly decreases to 0.142 cm3 g−1. As a result, the macropores will show a great influence on the interaction between the light and h-TiO2 NPs. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the PVP addition shows an impact on the light absorption below ca. 390 nm (band-gap of TiO2, 3.2 eV), while almost shows no influence on the light absorption within the visible region. The light absorption in the UV range decrease with increasing the PVP addition from 0 to 8 wt%. Once the PVP content exceeds 8 wt%, the UV light absorption reversely increase. Moreover, from the reflection spectra (Fig. S2), it is seen that the light reflectance in UV region first decreases with the increasing PVP content, while the light reflectance in visible region shows the minimum value at PVP addition of 10 wt%. Thus, combining with the absorption and reflection analysis, the h-TiO2-8 NPs benefit the solar light’s transmission. And further combining the above BET and BJH analysis, this increase in light transmission could be due to the increased volume of macropores. Thus, the increased light transmission would enhance the down-converted fluorescence effect from LPP layers, thereby improving the overall photovoltaic conversion efficiency of DSSCs devices.

Fig. 5(a) compares the J-V curves of DSSCs based on h-TiO2-0, h-TiO2-8 and h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes under AM 1.5 G illumination (100 mW cm−2). It is noted that in a typical photoanode, the thickness of h-TiO2 layers was set as ca. 12 µm, and that of LPP layers as ca. 25 µm (as illuminated in the inset of Fig. 5(a)). The DSSCs based on h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes show the highest photovoltaic performances. As listed in Table 1, the short-circuit current density (J SC) and PCE of h-TiO2-8 photoanodes is 13.34 mA cm−2 and 7.15%, which are 6.30% and 14.58% higher than those obtained for h-TiO2-0 ones (12.55 mA cm−2 and 6.24%), respectively. Here, it is noted that the SSA of h-TiO2-8 NPs is 26.25% higher than that of h-TiO2-0 ones. Then, these improvements in J SC and PCE of h-TiO2-8 NPs could be mainly due to the increased SSA. Moreover, the open-circuit voltages (V OCs) of h-TiO2-0 and h-TiO2-8 photoanodes are 0.750 V and 0.800 V, respectively. The value of V OC is determined by the difference between the Fermi energy level of the TiO2 photoanode (E f) and the redox potentials of the electrolyte (E r of I-/I3 -) [47–49]. And referred to the published literatures [50,51], the C doping in h-TiO2 NPs will cause a negative shift in their conduction band edge, accompanied by an up-shift in the Fermi energy level that results into an enhancement in the V OC of DSSCs. On the other hand, the fill factor (FF) almost kept unchanged after the PVP was added. Referred to the published literature [52,53], the FF mainly depends on the series resistance (R S) of DSSCs. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the intercept of the Nyquist plots at Z′ axis show that all photoanodes show a similar R S of ca. 12 Ω.

When incorporated a LPP layer into the h-TiO2 photoanodes, the J SC and PCE further significantly increase to 14.69 mA cm−2 and 8.05%, which are further improved by 10.12% and 12.59%, respectively. At the same time, the V OC further increases up to 0.824 V. This enhancement in V OC could be due to that the constructed TiO2/LPP interface can improve the separation of the photo-generated carriers that further enhance the V OC [19,47]. However the FF was almost kept unchanged, indicating that the incorporation of LPP layer almost shows no effect on the R S under assistance of its down-conversion fluorescence effect.

In order to fully understand the effect of macropores on the transport and recombination of photo-generated electrons in the h-TiO2 and h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes more comprehensively, Nyquist and Bode plots were tested. The Nyquist plots were tested under illumination of AM 1.5 simulated sunlight in the range of 0.01 Hz ~ 100 kHz. Typically, the left semicircle corresponds to the impedance (R 1) of the electrolyte I3 - ions during the oxidation-reduction reaction on the Pt counter electrode, the middle one to the charge transfer impedance (R 2) at the semiconductor material/electrolyte/dye interface, and the right one to the resistance (R 3) of the redox pair I-/I3 - during the Warburg diffusion processes in the electrolyte [54,55]. From Fig. 5(b), it is found that the R 2 of h-TiO2-8 photoanodes is ca. 17.78 Ω, which is smaller than that of h-TiO2-0 ones (23.56 Ω). This decrease in R 2 could be due to the existence of macropores and mesopores and their resultant interconnecting channels in the h-TiO2 films that effectively reduces the obstacle in the separation and transport of photo-generated electrons. Once the LPP layer was added, the R 2 further decreases to 11.81 Ω, which could be due to its effect of carriers’ separation. Moreover, the Bode plots can also prove this point of view (Fig. 5(c)). The f max of h-TiO2-0, h-TiO2-8, and h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes values are ca. 21.60, 12.13, and 8.26 Hz, respectively. And the lifetime (τ n) of the photo-generated electrons in the photoanodes can be estimated using the formula, τ n = 1/2πf max [24]. Then, the τ n of h-TiO2-0, h-TiO2-8, and h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes values were estimated to be ca. 72.7, 129.5, and 190.2 ms, respectively. These results indicate that the h-TiO2 NPs and the constructed h-TiO2/LPP interface can effectively increase the life of photo-generated electrons in DSSCs; that is to say that the hierarchical porous structures and the heterogeneous interface can effectively suppress the recombination of photo-generated electrons [20].

Besides, to further investigate the effects of macropores on the electrons’ lifetime (τ n) in DSSCs, open-circuit voltage-decay (OCVD) tests have been conducted, as shown in Fig. S3. All OCVD curves show a similar rapid response to the input simulated sunlight, but different decay rates after shutting off the light. And the h-TiO2-8 photoanodes show a much smaller decay rate than the h-TiO2-0 ones. When incorporated with a LPP layer, the voltage decay rate becomes even smaller, which could be due to the long persistence phosphor effect from LPP. And the electron lifetime (τ n) can also been estimated by fitting the OCVD curves using the following formula,
τ
n
= −
k
B
T
e
(
d
V
OC
d t
)
(where kT is the thermal energy and e is the unit charge) [56]. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the τ ns of h-TiO2-8 and h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes are much larger than that of h-TiO2-0 ones. At the voltage of 0.5 V, the τ ns were estimated to be ca. 0.186, 0.462, 0.763 s for h-TiO2-0, h-TiO2-8 and h-TiO2-8/LPP photoanodes. This comparison again proves that the macropores in h-TiO2 film can effectively improve the separation of photo-generated carriers and then suppress their recombination, and the h-TiO2/LPP/electrolyte composite interface can also benefit the separation of the photo-generated electrons [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization on PVP addition for h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: As stated above, the content of PVP addition greatly affects the porous structure and pore distribution of h-TiO2 NPs, which further change their light absorption and transmission that often show a direct influence on the cell performances of the resultant photoanodes. Thus, in this work, we have attempted a series of PVP contents, i.e., 0, 5, 8, 10, 15 wt%, and the thickness of the LPP layer was fixed at ca. 25 µm. As shown in Fig. 6(a) and listed in Table 2, both the J SC and PCE first monotonously increase with the increasing content of PVP addition, and reach their maximum values of 14.69 mA cm−2 and 8.05% at PVP content of 8 wt%, respectively; and then both of them gradually decrease with further increasing the PVP contents. On the other hand, the V OCs of all h-TiO2-n/LPP photoanodes almost keep unchanged (ca. 0.82 V). Referred to h-TiO2-0 and h-TiO2-8 photoanodes, the increase in V OCs of all h-TiO2-n/LPP photoanodes could be mainly due to the separation effect on photo-generated electrons from the constructed TiO2/LPP/electrolyte interface. Besides, the FF slightly increases from 64.81% to 67.09 with increasing PVP content from 0 to 10 wt%, and then quickly decrease with excessive PVP addition. This comparison implies that suitable PVP addition (8–10 wt%) can benefit the construction of porous structure and the doping of carbon in TiO2 that can improve the photovoltaic performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performances of h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes in dark
ce:section.ce:para: Different to other traditional DSSCs [2,4,12,48], the DSSCs based on h-TiO2/LPP-n photoanodes can still work after stopping illumination of solar light, which is due to the long persistence phosphor effect of the commercial SrAl2O4:Eu2+,Dy3+ particles [18]. As shown in Fig. 6(b), all DSSCs with a LPP layer can work in dark, while the DSSCs without a LPP layer stop work. As listed in Table 3, the DSSCs with h-TiO2-8/LLP photoanodes show the highest J SC of ca. 0.209 mA cm−2 in dark, which corresponds to the lowed light reflection among all h-TiO2-n NPs. And with a reference light power of LPP layer of ca. 175 μW cm−2 [20], the DSSCs with h-TiO2-8/LLP photoanodes show the highest PCE of ca. 57.37%, which is much larger than most of reported values [18,20,57]. On the other hand, the V OCs of the DSSCs with h-TiO2-8/LLP photoanodes decrease from ca. 0.82–0.67 V, which might be due to the recombination of photo-generated carries at the TiO2/FTO interface. Besides, the FFs show a small increase in dark, which is mainly due to the carbon doping in TiO2 that can reduce the resistance of TiO2 matrix.

In summary, a series of h-TiO2 NPs with tunable macropore volumes were designed via a two-step sol-gel hydrothermal process using PVP as pore-forming agent. Results show that the specific surface area, pore volumes and distribution can be well tuned by the PVP addition amounts, which further influence the dye molecules’ adsorption, incident light’s transmission, photo-generated electrons’ transport and electrolyte ions’ diffusion within the resultant h-TiO2/N719 photoanodes. The optimized DSSCs with h-TiO2 photoanodes show the maximum PCE of 7.15%, which is improved by 14.58% compared to the h-TiO2-0 ones. This increased PCE could be mainly due to the following two aspects: i) the macroscale pores or connecting channels between h-TiO2 particles that made by PVP increase the dye adsorption sites and dye load and improve the diffusion of electrolyte. ii) The carbon doping the lattice of h-TiO2 shift up the Fermi level that improve the V OC and increase the conductivity of h-TiO2 that benefit the charge transport and effectively inhibit the charge recombination at the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface. After incorporated with a LPP layer, the optimized DSSCs exhibit a PCE of 8.05%, which is further improved by 14.52% compared to the optimized h-TiO2 ones above. Series of tests and analysis indicate that this increase in PCE by LPP could be due to the following two aspects: i) the formation of macropores into h-TiO2 has improved the incident light’s entering the LPP layer for down-converting green phosphors; ii) the formed h-TiO2/LPP/electrolyte interface benefits the separation of photo-generated carriers. In addition, the optimized DSSCs with h-TiO2/LPP photoanodes can well work in dark and output the maximum J SC of 0.209 mA cm−2 with a real PCE of 57.37%. This study would provide some insight for the design of LPP-enhanced DSSC photoanodes for high-performance optoelectronic devices and the development of all-weather solar cells.

Shi Qiang Kang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. En Zi Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jun Di Cheng: Investigation. Xiu Yun Gu: Data curation. Kun Wei: Formal analysis. Geng Zhi Sun: Writing – review & editing. Xiu Ping Gao: Supervision. Xiao Jun Pan: Supervision. Jin Yuan Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Er Qing Xie: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos.: 11674140, 21701072, and 21975123), the Opened Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics (No. IOSKL2020KF24), the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (No. 20JR5RA237), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities in China (No. lzujbky-2020-63).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.164295.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of insulating oxides in blocking the charge carrier recombination in dye-sensitized solar cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An in-depth review on the role of carbon nanostructures in dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Janani, P. Srikrishnarka, S.V. Nair, A.S. Nair, An in-depth review on the role of carbon nanostructures in dye-sensitized solar cells, J. Mater. Chem. A, 3 (2015) 17914–17938.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoarchitectures in dye-sensitized solar cells: metal oxides, oxide perovskites and carbon-based materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.S. Shaikh, N.S. Shaikh, S.S. Mali, J.V. Patil, K.K. Pawar, P. Kanjanaboos, C.K. Hong, J.H. Kim, P.S. Patil, Nanoarchitectures in dye-sensitized solar cells: metal oxides, oxide perovskites and carbon-based materials, Nanoscale, 10 (2018) 4987–5034.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-doped titanum dioxide nanocrystals for highly efficient dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Lin, D. Qi, H. Ding, L. Wang, M. Xing, B. Shen, J. Zhang, Carbon-doped titanum dioxide nanocrystals for highly efficient dye-sensitized solar cells, Catal. Today, 281 (2017) 636–641.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced electron collection efficiency of nanostructured dye-sensitized solar cells by incorporating TiO2 cubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Sarvari, M.R. Mohammadi, Enhanced electron collection efficiency of nanostructured dye-sensitized solar cells by incorporating TiO2 cubes, J. Am. Ceram. Soc., 101 (2018) 293–306.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A double layered photoanode made of highly crystalline TiO2 nanooctahedra and agglutinated mesoporous TiO2 microspheres for high efficiency dye sensitized solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Yan, Y. Qiu, W. Chen, M. Zhang, S. Yang, A double layered photoanode made of highly crystalline TiO2 nanooctahedra and agglutinated mesoporous TiO2 microspheres for high efficiency dye sensitized solar cells, Energ. Environ. Sci., 4 (2011) 2168–2176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Y3Al5O12:Ce phosphors as a scattering layer for high-efficiency dye sensitized solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Zhu, X. Wang, H. Li, L. Pan, H. Sun, X. Liu, T. Lv, Z. Sun, Y3Al5O12:Ce phosphors as a scattering layer for high-efficiency dye sensitized solar cells, Chem. Commun., 48 (2012) 958–960.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mesoporous films prepared from synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles and their application in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Tasić, Z. Marinković Stanojević, Z. Branković, U. Lačnjevac, V. Ribić, M. Žunić, T. Novaković, M. Podlogar, G. Branković, Mesoporous films prepared from synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles and their application in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), Electrochim. Acta, 210 (2016) 606–614.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: TiO2 nanoparticles optimized for photoanodes tested in large area Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Hegazy, N. Kinadjian, B. Sadeghimakki, S. Sivoththaman, N.K. Allam, E. Prouzet, TiO2 nanoparticles optimized for photoanodes tested in large area Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC), Sol. Energ. Mat. Sol. C., 153 (2016) 108–116.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of the electron lifetime in nanocrystalline dye solar cells by open-circuit voltage decay measurements

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Zaban, M. Greenshtein, J. Bisquert, Determination of the electron lifetime in nanocrystalline dye solar cells by open-circuit voltage decay measurements, ChemPhysChem, 4 (2003) 859–864.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biomass converted carbon quantum dots for all-weather solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Meng, Y. Zhang, W. Sun, M. Wang, B. He, H. Chen, Q. Tang, Biomass converted carbon quantum dots for all-weather solar cells, Electrochim. Acta, 257 (2017) 259–266.
